items.title: Thaci Trial Verdict Will Decide Legacy of Kosovo Specialist Chambers
items.summary: The upcoming verdict in the war crimes trial of former Kosovo President Hashim Thaci and his co-accused will have enormous consequences for the defendants themselves, for the court, for Kosovo and for the future of transitional justice.

items.title: Greek Court Confirms Neo-Nazi Golden Dawn is ‘Criminal Organisation’
items.summary: Athens appeal court upholds guilty verdicts for members of prescribed extremist party, five-and-a-half years after the first-instance ruling.

items.title: Kosovo Detains Four Serbs Suspected of Wartime Killings
items.summary: Court orders detention of four local Serbs arrested on suspicion of killing six villagers in central Kosovo during the war in 1998.

items.title: Montenegro’s Arrest of Ex-Minister Reignites Arguments About ‘Selective’ Justice
items.summary: Former education minister’s arrest revives debate about the justice system’s shortcomings  – just as Montenegro seeks to prove its rule-of-law credentials to the EU in accession talks.

items.title: Lithium Mine in Czech Coal Heartland Exposes Flaws in EU’s Green Transition
items.summary: Supported by powerful interests under the banner of green transformation, a vast lithium project in Czechia’s former coal mining north-west is facing local opposition and throws into doubt the rationale of Europe’s eco-transition plans.

items.title: Serbian’s Childhood Flight from War Inspired Refugee Work Across the Globe
items.summary: In the fifth instalment of BIRN’s series about war-displaced people’s continuing links to the Balkans, Sandra Ivanov tells how her family’s emigration to New Zealand forged a commitment to humanitarian work.

items.title: Turkey Urges Israel to Release Detained Turkish Reporters
items.summary: Government, opposition and rights groups called on Israel to free two CNN Turkish journalists detained while doing a live broadcast from Tel Aviv.

items.title: ARTE Europe Weekly: European Air Defence Project’s Future in Doubt
items.summary: The future of a European fighter jet project is being threatened by disagreements between France and Germany.

items.title: Slovenian Campaigner Hails ‘Historic’ Advance for Abortion Rights in Europe
items.summary: Nika Kovac, head of a campaign for safe and free abortion in the EU, tells BIRN that European Commission approval for funding pregnancy terminations abroad for women who can’t access abortions in their home countries is a civil rights victory.

items.title: Share Your Experience: Child Support Payments After Divorce in the Balkans, France
items.summary: Do you live in the Balkans or France, and have you been awarded child support payments after divorce? Was the process easy – and did you receive the money as the court ordered?

items.title: Report Highlights Press Freedom’s Plight in Turkey, Serbia and Bulgaria
items.summary: New report says journalists’ safety is under ‘sustained pressure’ in Europe – with Turkey, Serbia and Bulgaria listed among the worst offenders.

items.title: Time Runs Short for Kosovo to Agree on New President
items.summary: The deadline for MPs to elect a new head of state expires soon, and a fresh election looms if they do not compromise on a name – or grant Vjosa Osmani a second term.

items.title: Orban Brings War into Hungarian Living Rooms
items.summary: Fidesz’s fearmongering using AI-generated war videos in the election campaign is cynical, harmful to children and often borders on the ludicrous. But will it work?

items.title: Chilling Effect: Serbia’s Growing Network of ‘Slippery’ Surveillance Technology
items.summary: BIRN has identified a growing network of increasingly sophisticated and intrusive surveillance technology in Serbia, in schools, hospitals, on the roads and in the air, all without an adequate regulatory framework.

items.title: Former Montenegrin President’s Brother in Custody Over Illegal Arms Stash
items.summary: Court orders detention for bank owner Aleksandar Djukanovic, brother of the country's former president, after police seized firearms, ammunition and ballistic vests at his family home.

items.title: Balkan Govts Scramble to Safeguard Citizens in Middle East Conflict Zone
items.summary: Some Balkan countries are planning to evacuate stranded citizens from the conflict zone around Iran, others have already managed to fly people out - although some have only issued security warnings.

items.title: Monday Briefing from the Balkans: March 2, 2026
items.summary: Catch up on the weekend’s most important developments with Balkan Insight’s digest of news from countries across the region.

items.title: Sea Power: Greek Shipping Navigates Choppy Waters to Retain its Supremacy
items.summary: Amid global political turbulence and tightening environmental regulations, Greek merchant shipping has kept its leading position – but not always without controversy.

items.title: Montenegro’s Crypto Ambitions Need Tough Rules to Succeed
items.summary: Montenegro is promoting the use of cryptocurrencies to signal its technological modernisation – but without better oversight, it risks becoming a permissive grey zone for illicit finance.

items.title: Albanians Pay Price of Legal Row over Wastewater Treatment Project
items.summary: For decades, sewage from the Albanian capital has been discharged untreated into the waters of the Lana River in Tirana.

items.title: Greeks Take to Streets to Commemorate Train Crash Victims
items.summary: Protesters have gathered in cities all over the country to remember the 57 victims of the Tempi train crash in 2023.

items.title: Montenegro Signs Deal With China to Extend Controversial Highway
items.summary: A Chinese consortium is to construct the second section of Montenegro's Bar-Boljare highway – whose first stretch was widely derided for leading 'from nothing to nowhere'.

items.title: Week in Review: Trades and Betrayals
items.summary: From European Union accession hopes to ongoing corruption investigations, our selection of Premium stories this week takes a look at the political wheeling and dealing going on behind the scenes in the region.

items.title: Albanian Prime Minister Under Pressure Dismisses Deputy Accused of Corruption
items.summary: After months of defending his deputy and Minister of Infrastructure, Belinda Balluku, who faces allegations of manipulating public tenders, Edi Rama finally dismissed her on Thursday night.

items.title: Democracy Digest: Hungary, Slovakia Step Up Pressure on Ukraine Over Oil Supplies
items.summary: Elsewhere, Poland’s political cohabitation under strain as institutional clashes meet coalition fragility; Czech lower house to debate lifting parliamentary immunity of Babis and Okamura; Slovak coalition is working on changes to postal voting rules.

items.title: How Kosovo’s War Shaped One Woman’s Girlhood – and Worldview
items.summary: ‘Girlhood at War’ by Vjosa Musliu is an account of coming of age during the 1998-99 Kosovo war – and how that has shaped her ideas about racial discrimination and the feeling of being ‘unwanted’.

items.title: Sarajevo Tram Protests Reflect Deeper Frustration With a Dysfunctional Bosnia
items.summary: Protesters took to the streets after a fatal tram accident - but their challenge is whether they can transcend Bosnia’s traditional identity politics in the name of a shared interest in social justice and putting an end to endemic corruption.

items.title: Serbia Spending More on Trump-Linked Lobbyists – to What Effect?
items.summary: Experts doubt the effect of Serbia’s spending on US lobbyists to burnish the country’s image in the eyes of Donald Trump.

items.title: Greek Court Finds Four Executives Guilty in Predator Spyware Case
items.summary: First-instance ruling sentences four company executives to 126 years in jail for involvement in illegal wiretapping using Predator spyware – and refers the case file to prosecution for further investigation into other suspects.

items.title: Bosnia’s Public Broadcaster Goes Blank For a Day Over Unpaid Debts
items.summary: BHRT's symbolic blank-screen broadcast on Thursday risks becoming permanent if its mountain of debt is not paid off by the end of this month.

items.title: Who Was Behind the Largest Cyberattack on Polish Energy Infrastructure in Years?
items.summary: Initial suspicion fell on Sandworm, the rowdy, sabotage-prone cyber wing of Russia’s GRU military intelligence. But the latest findings point to a different actor – and Europe should pay attention.

items.title: Letter from Washington: Buying Friends and Finding Common Foes
items.summary: As Milorad Dodik comes in from the cold, Albanians impress on Trump that they have an enemy in common.

items.title: Serbian Lawyers Strike, But EU Pressure Decisive in Opposing Judicial Changes
items.summary: Serbian lawyers stopped work for three days, but without a general strike in the judiciary, experts say only EU pressure can force the government to retract controversial legal amendments deemed “a serious step back”.

items.title: Extradited from Germany, Wartime Serb Commander Faces New Trial in Bosnia
items.summary: After his extradition from Germany, where he was serving a 28-year sentence imposed by the Hague Tribunal, Dragoljub Kunarac faces fresh proceedings in Bosnia for wartime killings and persecution.

items.title: Rallies for Ukraine Held in Balkans as Russia’s Invasion Enters Fifth Year
items.summary: Supporters of Ukraine gathered to show solidarity in Belgrade, Athens, Skopje and other cities in the Balkans as the fourth anniversary of Russia’s all-out military assault on its neighbour was marked.

items.title: Lessons Learned: It’s Time to Give Serbia the Undercover Treatment
items.summary: At a recent conference of the Global Investigative Journalism Network, one theme stood out for me personally as an investigative journalist in Serbia – the power of undercover reporting in societies where corruption is endemic and public trust shattered.

items.title: Romania’s Push to Trim Judges’ Gold-Plated Pensions Sparks Backlash
items.summary: Romania is moving to limit judges’ pensions as part of a wider reform of the nation’s troubled finances – but the judges weren’t giving up their lavish payoffs without a fight.

items.title: From Srebrenica to Melbourne: Survivors’ Children Keep Memory of Bosnia’s Genocide Alive
items.summary: In the fourth of BIRN’s series about war-displaced people’s enduring connections to their home countries, the founders of Children of Srebrenica Melbourne explain how they’re turning private grief into public commemoration.

items.title: Balkan Leaders Vow Solidarity with Ukraine on Anniversary of Invasion
items.summary: Croatian’s premier paid a visit to Kyiv alongside other EU leaders, while most Balkan leaders voiced strong support for Ukraine’s self-defence on the fourth anniversary of Russia’s military assault.

items.title: Kosovo Upholds Ex-Intelligence Chief’s Jail Sentence for Turkish Deportations
items.summary: Former intelligence agency chief Driton Gashi was sentenced to four years and eight months' prison following a retrial for his role in the highly controversial deportation of so-called 'Gulenists' to Turkey in 2018.

items.title: Outrage Voiced in North Macedonia Over Fugitive Ex-Deputy PM’s House Detention
items.summary: Decision to place Artan Grubi in house detention rather than prison, after he turned himself in following months on the run, sparks accusations of behind-the-scenes deals.

items.title: With Birth Rates in Free Fall, Czechia Faces Demographic Reckoning
items.summary: Once one of the continent’s demographic high achievers, the Czech Republic has, in just a few years, experienced a dramatic fall in its fertility rate – the 5th fastest in the world.

items.title: Republika Srpska Has a New President, But the Old One’s Still in Control
items.summary: Sinisa Karan is the new man signing the documents in Bosnia’s Serb-led Republika Srpska entity – but his predecessor, Milorad Dodik, remains the real decision-maker.

items.title: Hague Trial Puts Kosovo Guerrillas’ Claims of a ‘Just War’ Under Scrutiny
items.summary: Closing statements in the trial of Hashim Thaci and three other former Kosovo Liberation Army leaders have reignited controversy over allegations that the Hague-based war crimes court is effectively prosecuting the guerrilla force’s armed struggle.

items.title: Kosovo War-Crimes Verdict Against Serb Ex-Policeman Upheld
items.summary: Pristina court upholds initial 2024 sentence of 13 years' imprisonment for Serbian ex-policeman Dusko Arsic for committing war crimes in the village of Butovc.

items.title: Croatian President and Govt Wrangle over Israel Arms Sales
items.summary: As Defence Minister talks defence sector collaboration with Israel, President Milanovic reminds government that last year he ordered an end to all cooperation with the Israeli army, citing its 'unacceptable behaviour'.

items.title: Share Your Experience: Overtourism from Spain to Turkey and the Balkans
items.summary: Are you living in a hotspot destination? Struggling with rising rent in a popular neighbourhood or frustrated by the disruption caused by tourists? Tell us your story.

items.title: North Macedonia Arrests Fugitive Ex-Deputy Prime Minister
items.summary: Police at a border crossing on Monday seized former Deputy Prime Minister Artan Grubi – who has been a fugitive from justice for more than one year, accused of corruption.

items.title: Monday Briefing from the Balkans: February 23, 2026
items.summary: Catch up on the weekend’s most important developments with Balkan Insight’s digest of news from countries across the region.

items.title: Serbian President’s Suggestion of ‘Second-Tier’ EU Membership is a Betrayal
items.summary: Aleksandar Vucic’s Progressive Party came to power with pledges to lead Serbia into full EU membership. After 14 years of democratic backsliding, he now says he’s willing to settle for ‘second-tier’ status within the bloc.

items.title: Battery Makers: A Toxic Debate in Hungary’s Election
items.summary: Revelations about foreign battery makers in Hungary ignoring safety and environmental regulations and the government helping cover it up are all over the news. But how much the scandal will impact April’s election remains unclear.

items.title: Growing Pains: Albania’s Farmers Find Pathway to EU Markets Hard to Follow
items.summary: Albania’s huge agricultural sector is seeking to expand into European markets to flourish – but issues over old-fashioned methods, low productivity, banned pesticides and corruption stand in the way.

items.title: Turkey Urged to Free Deutsche Welle Journalist Arrested for ‘Disinformation’
items.summary: Deutsche Welle and media rights groups have called for the release of Alican Uludag, who was arrested for 'insulting the President' and 'disseminating disinformation' in a post on X.

items.title: Week in Review: Bad Omens and Tough Choices
items.summary: This week’s selection of Balkan Insight Premium stories dissects some of the challenges and difficult choices facing the region.

items.title: ARTE Europe Weekly: Is Europe Ready to Take on Ukraine’s War Effort – Alone?
items.summary: The US has pulled almost all its funding just as Ukraine is entering its fifth year of war. Can the European Union fill the gap?

items.title: Balkan Leaders Heap Praise on Trump’s Board of Peace at Ceremonial Launch
items.summary: Balkan and Turkish leaders lauded the Board of Peace at its Washington launch on Thursday – while the participation of an EU Commissioner from Croatia drew criticism from some EU states about her presence.

items.title: Democracy Digest: Orban Goes to Washington and Receives Trump’s Endorsement
items.summary: Elsewhere, Slovakia and Hungary blame Ukraine for lack of oil through pipeline apparently bombed by Russia; Polish bishop becomes first to stand trial over alleged failure to report sexual abuse; and finally, a new Czech environment minister.

items.title: ‘No Way Out’: Bosnia’s Domestic Violence Survivors Call for Better Protection
items.summary: Although stricter laws against gender-based violence have been introduced in Bosnia and Herzegovina, many cases never even reach a court – emboldening the abusers and leaving victims feeling helpless.

items.title: Rama is Turning Deputy’s Suspension Into ‘Personal Battle’ Against Albanian Judiciary
items.summary: Proposed legal amendments preventing courts from suspending senior officials would be a major blow to progress on tackling corruption - and to Albania’s EU accession process, writes legal expert Gentian Serjani.

items.title: As North Macedonia’s Ruling Party Revives Old Scandal, is Revenge the Motive?
items.summary: North Macedonia’s ruling VMRO DPMNE party has renewed attacks on the long-defunct Special Prosecution, its old foe in corruption cases. Analysts suspect its motivations are historical revisionism and retaliation.

items.title: Greek Court Increases Sentence for Abusive Priest, Founder of Children’s Shelter
items.summary: Athens court confirms guilty verdict for clerical founder of children's shelter, accused of abusing minors – and almost doubles the first-instance sentence.

items.title: Powerful Interests are Driving Digital Repression in Southeast Europe
items.summary: Digital spaces are the new frontlines for challenging entrenched political and economic interests, but the backlash against civic groups trying to expose corruption and environmental destruction or demand official accountability can be fierce.

items.title: End of an Era: Last Coal Mine in Czech Republic Closes
items.summary: In Stonava, miners emerge from the pit one last time as the sun sets on the Czech Republic’s 250-year coal era – a victim of shifting markets and Europe’s green transition.

items.title: Far-Right Croatian MP Sparks Crisis by Singing About Fascist Leader
items.summary: The Croatian Liberals have threatened to quit the governing coalition unless Prime Minister Andrej Plenkovic takes action to curb a far-right Homeland Movement MP who hymned a World War II-era fascist on video.

items.title: In Bosnia’s New Protest Wave, Youth is the Driving Force
items.summary: A fatal tram crash has galvanised a new generation of young protesters in Bosnia and Herzegovina who want genuine change and greater accountability from those in charge of a dysfunctional system.

items.title: On Moldova’s Border with Ukraine, Russian Drones Shatter Residents’ Nerves
items.summary: With no end to the four-year war in sight, Russian attacks on Ukraine are unsettling the eastern regions of neighbouring Moldova, where locals have repeatedly been disturbed by stray drones crashing.

items.title: Kosovo Ex-Guerrilla Leaders Say Justice Demands Acquittal at Trial’s End
items.summary: At the close of their trial in The Hague, the four former Kosovo Liberation Army leaders insisted on their innocence and voiced pride in what they recalled as a people's just struggle for freedom.

items.title: Bulgaria Schedules April Election as Interim Cabinet Assumes Power
items.summary: Interim government led by Andrey Gyurov takes office ahead of April elections, with polls predicting a strong showing by former President Rumen Radev’s as-yet-unnamed new party.

items.title: Slain Vukovar Defender’s Family Presses Serbia to Extradite Suspect
items.summary: French family of Croatian independence war fighter Jean-Michel Nicolier – who died in Vukovar in 1991 – urge Serbia to hand over a man they claim is the main suspect.

items.title: Serbian Police ‘Chase Down’ Protesters as Clashes Erupt in Belgrade Park
items.summary: Police are accused of 'chasing down and arresting' student protesters in a Belgrade park - the second outbreak of violence in the country this week after clashes erupted at another demonstration in the city of Novi Sad.

items.title: Children at Risk in Czechia: A System Struggling to Protect
items.summary: A mix of systemic problems and a culture that tolerates corporal punishment has led to a broader national failure in the Czech Republic to detect and prevent violence against children. Will a new law that came into effect on January 1 help rectify that?

items.title: Russian Despatch: Moscow is Losing Its Energy Grip on the Balkans
items.summary: In the first of his monthly despatches on Russian-Balkan relations, Maksim Samorukov assesses the impact of the sell-off of Gazprom and Lukoil’s businesses on Moscow’s interests and influence in the region.

items.title: ‘You Never Really Leave’: How Three Exiles Gave a Kosovo Village New Life
items.summary: In the third instalment of BIRN’s series about war-displaced refugees’ continuing links to the Balkans, three former journalists from Kosovo explain how they organised a literature festival to bring people back to a small village.

items.title: Serbian Prosecutors Launch Probe After ‘Thugs’ Attack Protest
items.summary: Protesters and journalists say they were attacked by hooded assailants at a demonstration held to coincide with a state ceremony attended by senior officials in the northern city of Novi Sad.

items.title: ‘Not in Our Name’: Kosovars on Statehood Day Protest Hague War Crimes Trial
items.summary: Thousands of people took to the streets of Pristina on the 18th anniversary of Kosovo’s declaration of independence to protest against the trial of four wartime guerilla leaders.

items.title: Decline of Greece’s Postal Service Causes Public Discontent
items.summary: The Greek public postal service is facing significant financial difficulties and a structural crisis - leaving some people across the country deprived of what they see as an essential service.

items.title: Bosnia Court Upholds Conviction of Wartime Fighters for Abusing Prisoners
items.summary: State court upholds verdict sentencing Slavisa Djeric to three years’ jail and Nenad Ujic to four, for the inhumane treatment of civilians detained in Rogatica in 1995.

items.title: Hungarian Election: Tisza Finally Lays Out its Vision for Change
items.summary: Lead opposition party Tisza has long been reticent about its policy plans. But now a new 240-page manifesto gives Hungarians a taste of what might await them if the party defeats Fidesz in April’s election.

items.title: Kurti’s Majority Does Not Guarantee ‘Easy Ride’ as Kosovo PM, Experts Warn
items.summary: Albin Kurti was smoothly confirmed as Prime Minister following his party’s election victory in December – but experts still foresee plenty of potential crises for him in office.

items.title: Mushroom Foraging is Big Business in Bosnia, But Often Out of Sight
items.summary: Mushroom foraging provides an income for thousands of people in Bosnia and Herzegovina, but much of the industry remains in a grey zone, where foragers work without contracts and exporters dodge forestry fees.

items.title: Kosovo Ex-Guerrilla Leaders’ Defence Claims Prosecution Failed to Prove Guilt
items.summary: The defence for the four former Kosovo Liberation Army leaders said in closing statements that the prosecution selectively used evidence, ignored historical facts – and failed to find witnesses linking them to the crimes.

items.title: Kosovo Canal Attack Suspects Plead Not Guilty to Terrorism Charges
items.summary: Three men accused of causing the November 2024 explosion on the Iber-Lepenc canal pleaded not guilty at the Basic Court in Pristina.

items.title: Albanian Ruling Party Proposes Law Granting Senior Officials Immunity
items.summary: Proposed legal changes, which would protect ministers and other top officials from suspension by the courts, draw condemnation as a move to block anti-corruption investigations.

items.title: Protesters Demand Accountability for Fatal Tram Crash in Bosnian Capital
items.summary: Protesters rallied in Sarajevo for a fourth consecutive day over a tram accident in which one person was killed and four others were injured.

items.title: Radio Free Europe’s Bulgaria, Romania Services to Close
items.summary: The closures follow that of RFE Hungary and reflect funding cuts by US Congress. The Moldovan service is secure for this year but external funding is being sought.

items.title: Monday Briefing from the Balkans: February 16, 2026
items.summary: Catch up on the weekend’s most important developments with Balkan Insight’s digest of news from countries across the region.

items.title: High-Profile Convict Escapes Embarrass Montenegro at Crucial EU Juncture
items.summary: In the space of a week, a convicted drug trafficker and a former special prosecutor have fled prison sentences in Montenegro, embarrassing the country just as it tries to convince the European Union of its law and order credentials.

items.title: When a Home Becomes a School: Recalling Kosovo’s ‘Unique’ Underground Classes
items.summary: Sixteen years old when his father handed over their house for the underground schooling of Kosovo Albanian children, Fadil Aliu has written a book celebrating the contribution of the parallel education system to Kosovo’s fight for freedom in the 1990s.

items.title: With No Peace in Sight, Europe’s Ukrainian Refugees Yearn for Home
items.summary: Four years after Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine began, some of the Ukrainians who fled the conflict are struggling to integrate in their host countries, while anti-refugee sentiments have risen across Europe.

items.title: Bulgaria Investigating Six Mountain Deaths as Group Suicide and Murder
items.summary: Authorities are investigating six deaths in two incidents in the Bulgarian mountains as a possible suicide-and-murder case linked to an alleged spiritual sect – amid mounting questions about institutional failures to act.
